notes:
chăng means “whether” and is used to show doubt or uncertainty. It often appears in indirect questions or when someone is wondering about something.

It usually comes at the end of a clause, after the verb or full idea. It sounds a bit formal or literary compared to everyday speech.

Very often it pairs with hay không to make the uncertainty clearer. In casual speech, people often just use không instead of chăng, so seeing chăng can feel more written or thoughtful.
exampleSentences: Anh không biết chăng em sẽ đến buổi tiệc tối nay.
Chị tự hỏi chăng trời sẽ mưa vào cuối tuần này.
Em chưa rõ chăng mình nên nhận lời mời đó hay không.

example.vi: Bạn có chắc là bạn sẽ đến chăng?
example.en: Are you sure you will come?

definitions.es: Se utiliza para expresar incertidumbre o para pedir confirmación.
definitions.en: Used to express uncertainty or to ask for confirmation.
definitions.vi: Dùng để diễn tả sự không chắc chắn hoặc để yêu cầu xác nhận.

exampleSentencesTranslated: I don't know whether you will come to the party tonight.
She wonders whether it will rain this weekend.
I'm not sure whether I should accept that invitation or not.
notes_md:
**chăng** means **“whether”** and is used to show doubt or uncertainty. It often appears in indirect questions or when someone is wondering about something.

It usually comes at the **end of a clause**, after the verb or full idea. It sounds a bit formal or literary compared to everyday speech.


Very often it pairs with **hay không** to make the uncertainty clearer. In casual speech, people often just use **không** instead of *chăng*, so seeing *chăng* can feel more written or thoughtful.
